preFix.title: Homemade marshmallows
preFix.subtitle: Soft, pillowy vanilla marshmallows cut into squares
preFix.excerpt: Homemade marshmallows are made by whipping hot sugar syrup into dissolved gelatine until the mixture triples in volume and turns white and billowing. Set in a tin, then cut into squares and dusted with icing sugar. The texture is entirely different from shop-bought: softer, more yielding, and considerably less sweet.
preFix.sourceNotes: Marshmallows have a history stretching back to ancient Egypt, where the root of the marsh mallow plant (Althaea officinalis) was used to make a sweetened confection. By the nineteenth century European confectioners had developed gelatine-based marshmallows using egg whites or gelatine as the aerating agent, and the industrial version was established in the late nineteenth century. Home versions using gelatine and sugar syrup appear widely in American domestic cookery books from the mid-twentieth century.

content.text: Marshmallow-making requires a stand mixer: the mixture must be whipped continuously for 8 to 10 minutes while it cools, and doing this by hand is not practical. The gelatine is dissolved in cold water first, then the hot sugar syrup is streamed in while the mixer runs. The mixture increases dramatically in volume as it cools and air is incorporated. By the time it is done, it should be white, thick, and slowly ribbon off the beaters.
Bloom the gelatine and cook the syrup

Line a 20 x 30 cm tin with baking paper and dust generously with half the icing sugar and cornflour mixture. Submerge 12 gelatine leaves in 150 ml cold water and leave for 5 minutes until soft. In a saucepan, combine 350 g caster sugar, 100 g golden syrup, and 100 ml water over a medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Then increase the heat and cook without stirring until the temperature reaches 127 degrees C on a sugar thermometer.

Squeeze out the bloomed gelatine and put it in the bowl of the stand mixer. Pour the hot sugar syrup over the gelatine in a steady stream with the whisk attachment running on low speed, then increase to high speed. Whisk for 8 to 10 minutes until the mixture is white, very thick, and has tripled in volume. Add 2 tsp vanilla extract in the final minute. Pour into the prepared tin, smooth the top, and dust with the remaining icing sugar and cornflour mixture. Leave at room temperature for 4 hours or overnight until fully set. Cut into squares with a sharp knife lightly oiled or dusted with icing sugar.

items.problem: The marshmallow is rubbery and dense rather than soft
items.solution: Too much gelatine or the mixture was not whipped long enough. Whisk for the full 8 to 10 minutes: the volume and colour change is significant. Less gelatine (10 leaves) gives a softer result.

items.problem: The marshmallow stuck to the tin and tore when removed
items.solution: The tin was not dusted generously enough with the icing sugar and cornflour mixture. Dust the tin heavily before pouring, and dust the top again before leaving to set.

tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
